_meta.description: SLA types, KPI timers, pause/resume behaviour, and escalation configuration.

mcq.q: Which component defines the maximum time allowed to resolve a case and triggers escalation actions when breached?
mcq.explanation: Service Level Agreements (SLAs) define KPIs such as First Response By and Resolve By times. When a KPI is about to be breached or is breached, SLA actions can send warnings or escalate the case automatically.
wrongExplanations: Entitlements control how many cases or hours of support a customer is entitled to — not the time targets for resolution.
Routing Rules determine which queue or agent receives a case — they do not enforce time targets.
Business Process Flows guide agents through stages of work — they do not set or enforce time-based targets.

mcq.q: What happens to an active SLA KPI timer when a case is put On Hold?
options: The timer continues counting — On Hold has no effect on SLA
The timer pauses and resumes from where it stopped when the case is reactivated
The timer resets to zero when the case is reactivated
The SLA is cancelled and must be manually reapplied
mcq.explanation: When a case status changes to a status reason configured as an On Hold status, the SLA KPI timer pauses. When the case is reactivated, the timer resumes from where it stopped — elapsed time before the hold is preserved.
wrongExplanations: SLA timers are directly affected by On Hold status — they pause to prevent unfair breach recording while the customer holds up progress.
The timer does not reset — it preserves elapsed time. Resetting would unfairly extend the SLA window.
The SLA is not cancelled — it resumes automatically when the case is reactivated.

dragdrop.instruction: Match each event in a case lifecycle to the corresponding SLA timer behaviour.
items: Case status set to On Hold
Case status set back to In Progress
zones: SLA timer resumes from where it stopped
SLA timer stops — KPI is met or missed
New SLA KPI may be applied depending on configuration

correctMapping.Case status set back to In Progress: SLA timer resumes from where it stopped
correctMapping.Case resolved: SLA timer stops — KPI is met or missed
correctMapping.Case reactivated after resolution: New SLA KPI may be applied depending on configuration

dragdrop.explanation: SLA timers start on case creation, pause on On Hold, resume on reactivation, and stop on resolution. Reactivated cases may trigger new SLA instances depending on the SLA configuration.

casestudy.title: Contoso Support Centre Setup
casestudy.scenario: Contoso has recently licensed Dynamics 365 Customer Service and is setting up the system from scratch. The support centre has the following requirements: 1. All inbound emails to support@contoso.com must automatically create cases 2. Cases must be categorised using a standard product taxonomy 3. Agents should only be able to edit cases they personally own 4. Supervisors must be able to view and reassign all cases across the team 5. Cases must be resolved within 4 hours of creation, with a warning at 3 hours 6. Agents must confirm the customer was notified before resolving a case The admin team is configuring all of these requirements before going live.

questions.q: A 4-hour resolution target with a warning at 3 hours must be configured. Which feature and KPI type achieves this?
options: Entitlement with a 4-hour total support time allocation
SLA with a Resolve By KPI set to 4 hours and a warning action at 3 hours
Business Process Flow with a 4-hour timer between stages
Routing Rule with an escalation condition after 3 hours
questions.explanation: An SLA with a Resolve By KPI of 4 hours is the correct configuration. SLA Actions can be configured to fire at a Warning time (3 hours) before the KPI expires, sending alerts or escalating the case.
wrongExplanations: Entitlements control the quantity of support (number of cases or hours a customer can request) — not time targets for resolution.
Business Process Flows do not have built-in timers that enforce resolution windows.
Routing Rules assign cases to queues based on attributes — they do not enforce time-based targets.
